primary.question: Under which circumstances can a concern or complaint be made under this University of Essex whistleblowing policy?
primary.expected_answer: A concern or complaint can be raised if it is in the public interest, i.e., it involves serious wrongdoing that has an impact beyond the individual making the disclosure.
primary.actual_answer:
Under the University of Essex whistleblowing policy, concerns can be raised if they involve serious wrongdoing that has an impact beyond the individual making the disclosure and is in the public interest. These disclosures should tend to show malpractice and must be made in accordance with the procedures outlined by the policy. Personal matters or financial/business decisions not involving malpractice are excluded from protection under this policy.

Sources:
- [https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/policy-whistleblowing.pdf]

judge.justification: The answer is mostly correct but includes unnecessary details about procedures and exclusions that are not relevant to the question asked.

follow_up.question: What happens after a concern is disclosed under this policy?
follow_up.expected_answer: After a concern is disclosed, the individual will typically make their disclosure to the University Secretary, who may then initiate an investigation. The individual making the disclosure will be kept informed of progress.
follow_up.actual_answer:
After a concern is disclosed under the University of Essex whistleblowing policy, the designated person will acknowledge the disclosure in writing within 10 working days. Depending on the nature of the disclosure, they may discuss next steps with People and Culture or another senior independent member of staff. Possible next steps include:

- An investigation under the University’s Conducting Investigations Guidance.
- An 'ad hoc' investigation if needed.
- Referral to the police.
- No further action if the concern is judged to be without merit.

The outcome will be reported to the designated officer, who will consider what further actions to take, such as referral under the Disciplinary Procedure for the individual involved. The individual raising the concern will be kept informed of progress, while maintaining confidentiality where necessary.

Sources:
- [https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/policy-whistleblowing.pdf]

follow_up.question: What are the stages involved in raising a complaint under this procedure?
follow_up.expected_answer: The stages involve Early Resolution and Formal Complaint. Students are directed to the next stage if they remain dissatisfied at the conclusion of the Early Resolution stage.
follow_up.actual_answer:
The stages involved in raising a complaint under this procedure are as follows:

1. **Early Resolution** (see Section 4)
   - Seeks to resolve concerns swiftly and effectively at the point at which the concern is raised, or as close to that point as possible.
   - Concerns may be made in writing or at a meeting with relevant University staff.

2. **Formal Complaint** (see Section 5)
   - Appropriate where a student is dissatisfied with the outcome of early resolution, or where early resolution is not possible.
   - Usually investigated by an independent Complaint Investigator appointed by the Student Progress Team.
   - May include mediation or conciliation where appropriate.

3. **Internal Review** (see Section 7)
   - Provides an opportunity for a formal Internal Review, carried out by the Pro-Vice-Chancellor (Education) (or nominee), to ensure that appropriate procedures were followed and/or that the outcome was reasonable.
   - This stage will not normally require a reconsideration of the issues raised.

4. **External Review** (see Section 8)
   - Where the complainant remains dissatisfied, they can submit a request for a review by the Office of the Independent Adjudicator (OIA) within twelve months of the date of the Completion of Procedures letter provided by the University.
   
Sources:
- [source_url: https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/student-concerns-complaints-procedure.pdf]

The University of Essex ensures that core values such as academic freedom and freedom of speech are promoted in several ways:

1. **Tolerance of Diverse Ideas**: The University fosters an environment where a wide range of ideas and opinions, which are lawfully expressed, can be shared through various means including speech, writing, images, or events held on campus.

primary.question: What are the conditions for a student to proceed to the final year if they have passed fewer than 120 credits in Year One?
primary.expected_answer: A student may be allowed to proceed to the Final Year, following an attempt at reassessment, having passed a minimum of 105 credits, provided they have passed all the core modules for the Year.
primary.actual_answer:
Based on the provided context, the conditions for a student to proceed to the final year if they have passed fewer than 120 credits in Year One vary depending on how many credits were failed:

- If you have failed 30 or more credits in Year One:
  - You must pass 120 credits in the Final Year (source_url: roa-ug-3yr-final-year-rules.pdf, roa-ug-3yr-year-1-rules.pdf).

- If you have failed 15 credits in Year One:
  - Following reassessment, failure may be condoned in up to 15 non-core credits in the Final Year (source_url: roa-ug-3yr-final-year-rules.pdf, roa-ug-3yr-year-1-rules.pdf).

- If you passed all 120 credits in Year One:
  - Failure may be condoned in up to 30 non-core credits in the Final Year (source_url: roa-ug-3yr-final-year-rules.pdf, roa-ug-3yr-year-1-rules.pdf).

For Foundation Degrees:
- If you have failed 15 or more credits in Year One:
  - You must pass 120 credits in the Final Year (source_url: roa-ug-foundation-degrees-year1.pdf, roa-ug-foundation-degrees-year2.pdf).

- If you have failed fewer than 15 credits in Year One:
  - Failure may be condoned in up to 15 non-core credits in the Final Year (source_url: roa-ug-foundation-degrees-year1.pdf, roa-ug-foundation-degrees-year2.pdf).

Sources:
- [roa-ug-3yr-final-year-rules.pdf](https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/directories/academic-section/rules-of-assessment/ug/current/3-year-honours-degrees/roa-ug-3yr-final-year-rules.pdf)
- [roa-ug-3yr-year-1-rules.pdf](https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/directories/academic-section/rules-of-assessment/ug/current/3-year-honours-degrees/roa-ug-3yr-year-1-rules.pdf)

primary.question: What are the general principles for reassessment according to the rules of assessment document?
primary.expected_answer: The general principles for reassessment apply throughout all years, including Year One, Two, Three, and the Final Year, but specific details vary by year.
primary.actual_answer:
According to the provided documents, the general principles for reassessment include:

- If a student has failed any module(s) after completing all assessments but has achieved marks of 20 or above in all other modules undertaken, they must take reassessment before progressing to the next stage or year (e.g., see sections from [iep-portobello-year-2.pdf], [iep-portobello-year-3.pdf], and [roa-ug-3yr-year-1-rules.pdf]).

- The Board of Examiners can permit up to two further attempts at any module. However, your Module Mark(s) for those further attempts will be capped at 40 unless you have substantiated Extenuating Circumstances (see [chula---original-course-structure.pdf]).

- Your previous higher mark is used by the Board of Examiners to determine whether or not you can progress if you attempt reassessment (e.g., see sections from [iep-portobello-year-2.pdf], [iep-portobello-year-3.pdf], and [roa-ug-3yr-year-1-rules.pdf]).

- Trailing reassessment may be offered for students with Tier 4 or Student Route visas, but some options might not be available due to Home Office rules and guidance (e.g., see sections from [iep-portobello-year-2.pdf], [chula---original-course-structure.pdf], and [roa-ug-3yr-year-1-rules.pdf]).

Sources:
- https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/directories/academic-section/rules-of-assessment/ug/current/partner-institutions/chula---original-course-structure.pdf
- https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/directories/academic-section/rules-of-assessment/ug/current/partner-institutions/iep-portobello-year-2.pdf
- https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/directories/academic-section/rules-of-assessment/ug/current/partner-institutions/iep-portobello-year-3.pdf
- https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/directories/academic-section/rules-of-assessment/ug/current/partner-institutions/roa-ug-aegean-omiros-4yr-non-standard-year-2.pdf
- https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/directories/academic-section/rules-of-assessment/ug/current/3-year-honours-degrees/roa-ug-3yr-year-1-rules.pdf
- https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/directories/academic-section/rules-of-assessment/ug/current/3-year-honours-degrees/roa-ug-3yr-year-2-rules.pdf

follow_up.question: What are the compulsory reassessment opportunities at the end of Year One according to the document?
follow_up.expected_answer: Compulsory reassessment opportunities at the end of Year One are provided after students have completed their assessments and before the resit assessment period.
follow_up.actual_answer:
According to the provided documents, the compulsory reassessment opportunities at the end of Year One include:

- If you do not pass the Year but have a Year Mark of 20 or above, you will be offered reassessment.
- If you fail up to 60 credits, you will be offered compulsory reassessment before the start of the next Year. Your Module Mark(s) will be capped at 40.
- If you fail more than 60 credits, you will be offered one or more of the following options.

These rules are consistent across multiple documents for the academic year 2025-26 from different partner institutions with similar structure and wording. Sources:
- https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/directories/academic-section/rules-of-assessment/ug/current/partner-institutions/roa-ug-aegean-omiros-3yr-non-standard-year-1.pdf
